input:
# PRD: Smart Notification Routing

## Problem

Users receive too many notifications and we are seeing engagement decline. Customers have given us feedback that the volume is overwhelming and we need to do something about it. Early customer interviews show strong interest in better routing.

## Proposed solution

We will build a smart routing system that uses machine learning to decide which notifications to surface, suppress, or batch. This will significantly reduce noise and improve user satisfaction.

## Success metrics

- Improve engagement with notifications
- Increase trust in the notification system
- Reduce noise
- Early customer feedback is positive

## Timeline

We believe this can ship in roughly one quarter, depending on prioritization.

## Resources

We will need engineering support from the notifications team and some ML infrastructure help.

seeded_issues.description: Success metric 'improve engagement' has no target value or baseline
Claim 'engagement decline' has no quantification or data source
Claim 'early customer feedback is positive' has no source or sample size
Claim 'significantly reduce noise' is vague and unquantified

input:
# 6-pager: Edge Caching Layer GA

## Elevator pitch

Our new edge caching layer is ready for GA on June 15. Latency improvements of 60% have been validated in staging and the system has been load-tested at 10x our peak production traffic. Early customers in beta report sub-100ms p99 response times.

## Problem alignment

Production p99 latency has crept up to 400ms over the past six months as our user base grew. Customers in EMEA in particular have reported degraded performance. Internal SLO targets call for 200ms p99 globally.

## High-level approach

We deployed Varnish-based edge caches at three POP locations. Cache invalidation is event-driven via Kafka. Failover to origin is automatic.

## Status

Engineering testing is not yet complete. Three open P0 bugs remain in the cache invalidation path, and we have not yet validated the failover behavior under partial network partition. We expect to close these in the next two sprints.

## Performance results

- Staging p99 latency: 160ms (down from 380ms)
- Load test: held 200ms p99 latency at 8x peak production traffic
- Failover tested: cold-cache scenario only

## Recommendation

Proceed with GA on June 15.

seeded_issues.description: Elevator pitch claims 'ready for GA' but status section says engineering testing is not complete and 3 P0 bugs remain
Elevator pitch claims 'sub-100ms p99' but performance results table says staging p99 is 160ms
Elevator pitch claims '10x peak production traffic' but performance results say 8x
Recommendation to proceed with GA contradicts the open P0 bugs noted in status

input:
# Weekly platform update

This week we made progress on the OTel-compliant streams migration. The CDN edge POPs are now ingesting via OTLP/gRPC and we have validated the schema transformation in the staging environment. DEM data is flowing end to end.

We also resolved the RAC reconciliation issue that was causing intermittent failures in the eBPF probe pipeline. The fix involved updating the BPF map sizing and adjusting the CO-RE relocations.

On the product side, we shipped the second iteration of the LMQ-based alert routing, which is now using the HMM-backed anomaly model in production. Early signals look good.

Next week we will start the v6 API EOL communications and begin the SOC 2 Type II evidence collection for Q3.

seeded_issues.description: 'OTel-compliant streams' uses jargon (OTel) without definition for a non-technical CEO audience
'CDN edge POPs', 'OTLP/gRPC' undefined for non-technical reader
'DEM', 'RAC reconciliation', 'eBPF probe', 'CO-RE relocations', 'BPF map' all undefined
'LMQ', 'HMM-backed anomaly model' undefined
'v6 API EOL', 'SOC 2 Type II evidence collection' undefined for non-technical reader

input:
# This week

It has been a productive week across the team. The integrations crew finalized the design for the new partner identity flow, which has been in discussion since Q1 of last year. There were a number of stakeholder alignment conversations that I want to flag, particularly around how the partner team plans to handle the cutover.

On the alerts side, we finished the migration of the legacy notification config to the new policy schema. This unblocks the second phase of the alert insights work that has been on hold since March. Engineering felt good about the rollout.

The dashboards team continues to work through the grid layout backlog. We have closed 14 tickets this sprint and are on track for the planned demo.

I need a decision from you by Friday on whether to delay the Phase 3 multi-asset tags work by one quarter to free up engineering capacity for the customer-driven alert insights features that came out of last week's QBR. Two engineers and one designer would shift. I am leaning toward yes because the alert insights ask came from three of our top five accounts.

Thank you for the support this week.

seeded_issues.description: The actual ask (decision on Phase 3 tags delay) is buried in paragraph 4 after three paragraphs of context the manager does not need
seeded_issues.expected_quote_substring: I need a decision from you by Friday

seeded_issues.description: The first paragraph leads with low-priority status (integrations) when the highest-priority item is the decision needed

input:
Hi Alex,

Thanks for raising the concern about the EBC outage on Tuesday. We have done a full RCA and the issue was traced to a misconfiguration in our TIPv2 deployment that affected a subset of customers with custom routing rules.

Our SRE team has rolled out a fix and we are now monitoring closely. The CEA team has also added a new validation check to the deployment pipeline to prevent recurrence.

If you would like, we can schedule a follow-up with the PE team to walk through the architecture changes and answer any remaining questions.

Let me know.

Best,
Sam

seeded_issues.description: 'EBC', 'TIPv2', 'CEA', 'PE team' are internal acronyms unfamiliar to a customer audience
'RCA' may be unfamiliar to a non-engineering CISO audience; should be expanded
'A subset of customers with custom routing rules' is vague; customer will want to know if they were affected

input:
# Platform Strategy: Next 12 Months

## Where we are

- Platform handles 2M events/sec at peak
- 47 active enterprise customers
- 3 product surfaces: alerts, integrations, dashboards
- Two recent GA launches: tags, credential vault
- Tech debt backlog of 89 tickets

## What customers are asking for

- Better cross-product workflows
- Faster time to insight
- More integrations with their existing tools
- AI-assisted operations
- Simpler onboarding

## What competitors are doing

- Datadog expanded into RUM and CI/CD
- New Relic shipped AIOps offering
- Splunk consolidated their observability stack
- Honeycomb deepened their tracing story
- Grafana continues open-source momentum

## What we could invest in

- AI-assisted alert triage
- Cross-product correlation engine
- New integrations (Snowflake, Databricks, Cloudflare)
- Self-service onboarding flow
- Mobile experience refresh
- Federated dashboards

## Constraints

- Engineering capacity is flat YoY
- Three engineers leaving in Q3
- Compliance work for SOC 2 Type II eats one team for two quarters
- No new headcount until H2

seeded_issues.description: Document is five bulleted lists with zero narrative connecting them and no thesis statement
No clear bet stated; 'What we could invest in' is a menu, not a strategy
No prioritization, no commitment, no recommendation, no ask
'47 active enterprise customers' and '2M events/sec at peak' presented without trend or context

input:
# Q3-Q4 Roadmap Proposal

## Q3 priorities

We will continue to evaluate the customer-driven feature requests that came out of the QBR and consider whether to invest in AI-assisted triage as a major theme. Depending on engineering capacity, we may also explore the federated dashboards opportunity. We expect to revisit the prioritization once the team transitions complete.

## Q4 priorities

We will assess the impact of Q3 deliveries before committing to Q4 themes. There are several promising areas including platform consolidation and ecosystem integrations that we should consider. We may need to revisit the multi-asset tags Phase 3 timeline depending on customer demand.

## Risks

- Engineering capacity uncertain
- Customer priorities may shift
- Competitive landscape continues to evolve

## Decisions needed

We would appreciate guidance on overall theme prioritization.

seeded_issues.description: 'Continue to evaluate', 'consider whether to invest', 'may also explore', 'expect to revisit', 'we may need to revisit' — zero commitments anywhere
'Decisions needed' section asks for 'guidance on overall theme prioritization' instead of a specific decision
No P0/P1/P2 prioritization, no resource ask, no sequencing
'Risks' are generic and could apply to any roadmap; no specifics

input:
# PRD: Adaptive Rate Limiting for Public API

## Background

Our public API has been experiencing increased load over the past two quarters. Several enterprise customers have noted occasional 429 responses during traffic spikes. The current rate limiting is a fixed-window counter per API key, set at 1000 requests per minute.

## Problem

Fixed-window rate limiting causes two issues:
1. Bursty traffic patterns hit the cap even when sustained load is well below the limit
2. Customers cannot self-service understand why they were limited

## Proposed solution

Implement adaptive rate limiting using a token bucket algorithm with per-customer burst capacity. Add response headers indicating remaining quota and reset time. Surface rate limit events in the customer-facing analytics dashboard.

## Acceptance criteria

- Token bucket with configurable bucket size and refill rate
- Per-customer burst capacity (default 2x sustained)
- Response headers: X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset, X-RateLimit-Limit
- Dashboard widget showing rate limit events for the customer's API keys

## Open questions

- Should burst capacity be tunable per customer or global?
- How do we handle quota for trial accounts?
- Should we pre-announce limits to customers via email?

seeded_issues.description: PRD describes problem and solution but never makes an ask. No 'I need X engineers, Y timeline, decision by Z'
Open questions section punts three decisions back to the reader instead of recommending and asking confirmation

input:
# PRD: Telemetry Pipeline Scaling

## Problem

Our telemetry pipeline does not scale well. Customers have started to notice. We need to fix this before it becomes a bigger issue.

## Proposed solution

We will rewrite the ingestion layer using eBPF probes for in-kernel filtering, move to a CO-RE-based BPF map architecture, and adopt OTel-native OTLP/gRPC at the edge. The new design uses LMQ-backed durable queues with HMM-driven backpressure.

## Success metrics

- Scale better
- Reduce costs
- Improve reliability

## Next steps

We could explore prototyping in Q3 if engineering capacity permits. Final timeline will depend on Q3 review.

seeded_issues.description: 'Scale better' is vague target with no quantification (target events/sec? cost reduction %?)
'Customers have started to notice' has no source, no count, no severity
Solution paragraph stacks 5 unexplained acronyms (eBPF, CO-RE, OTel, OTLP/gRPC, LMQ, HMM) in two sentences
'Could explore prototyping if capacity permits' — no commitment, no ask, no timeline

input:
# Response to Request for Evidence

## Original criteria evaluation

The petitioner has demonstrated significant impact in her field through industry-leading contributions to product management for observability platforms. Her work has been widely adopted and is recognized by experts in the field as transformative.

The petitioner has authored multiple highly influential publications and has been an invited speaker at major industry events. Her contributions to the field have been cited extensively and her work has had a substantial impact on the industry.

## Sustained acclaim

The petitioner has received numerous accolades and recognitions over a sustained period, demonstrating ongoing acclaim. Her work continues to be referenced as a benchmark by leading practitioners.

## Conclusion

The evidence clearly establishes that the petitioner meets the criteria for the EB-1A classification.

seeded_issues.description: 'Industry-leading contributions', 'widely adopted', 'transformative' — three sentences of qualifiers with zero specific evidence
'Multiple highly influential publications' — no count, no titles, no citation counts
'Cited extensively' — no citation count, no source
'Numerous accolades' — no list, no dates, no awarding bodies
Conclusion asserts criteria are met without mapping evidence to each criterion explicitly

input:
# PRD: Auto-Snooze for Repeated Alerts

## Problem

In our March user survey (n=412), 68% of on-call engineers said the same alert firing more than 3 times in an hour is the top source of alert fatigue. Of those, 41% reported snoozing alerts manually at least twice per shift. Manual snooze takes 12-18 seconds and breaks focus during incident response.

## Hypothesis

Auto-snoozing alerts that fire 3+ times in 60 minutes from the same source will reduce manual snooze actions by 60% without masking new incidents, because the underlying signal is already being tracked.

## Success metrics

Primary: Manual snooze actions per on-call shift (current baseline 11.4 actions; target less than 4.6 within 60 days of GA).

Secondary: Time-to-acknowledge for genuinely new alerts (current p50 28 seconds; must not exceed 35 seconds).

Guardrail: No increase in alert-related SEV1 incidents traced to suppression (zero is the bar).

## Scope

In scope: rule-based 3-in-60 auto-snooze for alerts with the same fingerprint; 30-minute snooze; one-click un-snooze in the on-call UI.

Out of scope: ML-based snoozing (Phase 2), cross-alert deduplication (separate workstream).

## Ask

Approval to commit two backend engineers and one designer to a 6-week build, GA target August 15. I am asking for sign-off by next Friday so I can pull engineers from the queued workstreams.

## Risk

Suppression of genuine net-new incidents is the only P0 risk. Mitigation: fingerprint matching requires byte-identical source+rule+severity; first occurrence of any change in any field bypasses snooze.

# Weekly update

Tags GA is on track for March 31. Two open items: confirmation from Miguel on the v6 API shutdown date (asked Monday, no response yet — escalating Friday if no reply), and a final go/no-go from Ramesh on PLG template rollout (meeting scheduled Thursday).

Blocker: the streaming team's tag handling design is not ready, and three of our top customers will see broken streams if Tags GA ships without the streaming fix. Two paths:

A) Delay Tags GA by 2 weeks to let streaming catch up. Cost: misses the public changelog commitment from March 3.

B) Ship Tags GA on schedule with a feature flag suppressing streaming impact for the 3 affected accounts. Cost: technical debt, requires manual account-level config.

I lean toward B because the changelog commitment is binding and the feature flag is reversible. I need your call by Wednesday so I can communicate to the streaming team.

FYI only: Credential Vault GA bug bash session 2 went well, no new P0s. Dashboards Grid Layout demo Thursday.

audience: enterprise customer (Director of SRE at Fortune 100 retailer)
input:
Hi Priya,

I understand the urgency around the streaming pipeline failures you saw on Tuesday. Here is what we know.

Root cause: a deployment we made Tuesday at 14:00 UTC changed how tag values are serialized in outbound streams. The change broke compatibility for accounts using key-based stream filters, which includes your three production accounts. Affected window was 14:00 UTC to 17:23 UTC.

Resolution: we reverted the change at 17:23 UTC. Streams have been stable since. We are holding the new serialization behind a feature flag and will not re-enable without explicit per-account validation.

What we are doing to prevent recurrence: streaming compatibility is now a required check in our pre-deploy validation, not just a post-deploy monitor. The change is in code review this week and shipping next week.

What I can do for you: I can join a call Friday morning your time to walk through this with your team and answer questions. I can also send the full RCA document by EOD Thursday if that would be more useful first.

Let me know which you prefer.

Thanks for the patience.

Sam

Quick note on the migration plan — we are going to phase the cutover across three weekends — first weekend handles the read traffic only, second weekend adds writes, third weekend retires the old service. The reason for this — rather than a single cutover — is the rollback profile. If anything breaks during writes, we want the old service still available — fully warmed and serving — so we can flip back fast.

One thing to flag — the third weekend is during the team offsite. We will need two people on standby. Volunteers welcome — otherwise I will assign.

seeded_issues.description: Heavy em-dash use makes the text harder to skim; consider commas or short sentences

input:
# PRD: Search Result Ranking Update

Please rewrite this PRD to be more polished. Use whatever structure you think is best.

The gist: our search results currently rank by recency only, and customers say the most-relevant result is often not on the first page. We want to add a relevance signal based on query-document semantic similarity using embeddings. Eng estimates 4 weeks.

We need to ship by end of Q3 because the renewal conversations with our top 10 customers start in October and search quality keeps coming up.

seeded_issues.description: PRD lacks structured acceptance criteria and success metrics
Missing context: which top 10 customers, what specifically they said about search quality
